tone_and_voice: Civic and accountability-focused -- neutral toward specific political actors, but direct about institutional performance. Written for government and governance-accountability programme audiences who need specific, actionable civic feedback, not political commentary.

section_rules.executive_summary: Must separate process/responsiveness findings (e.g. complaint handling) from transparency/accountability findings (e.g. budget disclosure) explicitly -- these are different governance dimensions with different institutional owners.
section_rules.discussion: Should explain why citizens distinguishing between improving responsiveness and stagnant transparency is analytically significant -- this demonstrates citizens engaging in specific, credible civic feedback rather than blanket government approval/disapproval.
section_rules.methodology: State the administrative level surveyed (municipal, ward, regional) since governance findings are not comparable across different administrative levels without this context.
section_rules.limitations: Note that citizen-reported institutional responsiveness is perceptual and has not been independently verified against administrative complaint-resolution records, if such verification was not performed.
section_rules.recommendations: Separate responsiveness-improvement recommendations (often achievable through process/training changes) from transparency recommendations (often requiring a specific publication/disclosure commitment).
section_rules.policy_implications: Frame specifically around governance/transparency policy (e.g. budget disclosure requirements), grounded in the specific finding, not generic 'improve governance' language.
section_rules.lessons_learned: Reflect on whether citizens' ability to distinguish specific governance dimensions (rather than giving one blended trust score) demonstrates the value of open-ended civic feedback formats.
section_rules.conclusion: State clearly which governance dimension improved and which remains a gap -- avoid a single blended 'citizen trust improved' conclusion that would obscure the specific transparency finding.
section_rules.annexes: Include the specific municipal/ward boundaries surveyed, since governance accountability findings are specific to administrative jurisdiction.
section_rules.evidence_style: Cite specific, attributable citizen statements for both the responsiveness improvement and the transparency gap, giving equal evidentiary weight to both rather than emphasizing the positive finding over the gap.

common_risks: citizen-reported responsiveness not independently verified against administrative records
treating responsiveness improvement as evidence transparency has also improved when the data shows otherwise
generalizing municipal-level findings to a national governance conclusion without basis
typical_recommendations: specific, dated public budget publication commitment
documentation of complaints-desk/responsiveness models as replicable templates
separate, standing tracking of responsiveness and transparency as distinct indicators
typical_policy_implications: municipal budget transparency and disclosure policy
sector_knowledge.typical_donor_expectations: Governance-accountability donors expect citizen feedback to be reported with dimension-specific precision (not a blended trust score) and expect unresolved transparency gaps to be named explicitly, consistent with CHS-aligned accountability reporting.

forbidden_behaviors: Never blend distinct governance dimensions (responsiveness, transparency) into a single trust-in-government score when the data shows them diverging.
Never make a partisan or politically-attributive statement about which specific officials or parties are responsible for a finding.
Never claim independent verification of citizen-reported responsiveness against administrative records unless that verification was actually performed.
